romania.title: PMB anunță prelungirea metroului de la Păcii / Preciziei spre Centură, Chiajna și Cartierul Latin. Protocol aprobat de CGMB
romania.summary: Primăria Capitalei anunţă, miercuri, că magistrala de metrou M3, Anghel Saligny - Preciziei, va fi prelungită, în vestul Capitalei, spre Centura Capitalei, Chiajna şi Cartierul Latin. Potrivit sursei citate, municipalitatea Bucureştiului se asociază cu Metrorex, Primăria Sectorului 6 şi Ministerul Transporturilor pentru realizarea lucrărilor. Protocolul a fost aprobat miercuri de consilierii generali, la iniţiativa primarului Sectorului 6, Ciprian Ciucu.

romania.title: Eroul lui Alex. Mărturia lui Mihail Soare, salvatorul care l-a zărit pe copil cu coada ochiului, din elicopter: Nici nu putea să plângă
romania.summary: Alex, copilul dispărut în pădurea din județul Sibiu și căutat ore întregi de sute de salvatori, a fost găsit în viață după ce un membru al echipajului elicopterului care survola zona a observat „cu coada ochiului” ceva albastru într-un luminiș. Mihail Soare, salvatorul care a participat la intervenție, a povestit într-un interviu acordat Digi24 cum o verificare făcută cu camera cu termoviziune a confirmat că este vorba despre băiatul dispărut.

romania.title: Tur ghidat organizat de Fundația Calea Victoriei | Descoperă Bucureştiul Art Deco şi arhitectura modernistă

romania.summary: Elevii de clasa a II-a au început în această săptămână Evaluarea Națională 2026, odată cu susținerea probei scrise la Limbă și comunicare, organizată marți, urmată miercuri de testarea competențelor de citire. Probele s-au desfășurat în timpul orelor de curs și au avut o durată de 45 de minute.

Problema este însă următoarea: toți acești olteni vor rămâne definitiv în oraș după terminarea meciului. După ce că deja mai mulți olteni decât și-ar […] The post Vești proaste pentru Sibiu! Câteva mii dintre zecile de mii de angajați CFR au muncit o noapte întreagă și au uns cu slănină toate […] The post CFR a dublat viteza trenurilor după ce a uns toate șinele cu slănină appeared first on Times New Roman .

Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

romania.summary: United Way România lucrează acolo unde nevoia este cea mai mare, acolo unde sprijinul nu este doar util, ci vital. Fără acest ajutor, mulți copii ar rămâne în urmă, riscând să se piardă în dificultățile vieții. De aceea, apreciem sprijinul pe termen lung pe care Raiffeisen Bank îl oferă programului „Învață să reușești”. Printre copiii […] Articolul Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești” apare prima dată în United Way Romania .

medical.title: Living 'tumor on a chip' could give best ever insight into aggressive brain cancer
medical.summary: Scientists are creating a glioblastoma 'tumor on a chip'—a tiny living system capable of mimicking the key features of the human brain and providing a deeper understanding of how the aggressive brain cancer works.

medical.title: Alzheimer's therapeutic advancements may be financially inaccessible
medical.summary: A newly published study shows that while disease-modifying therapies (DMTs) may offer substantially greater health benefits than usual care, their real-world impact will depend on whether they are priced in ways that health systems can afford.

medical.title: How songbirds learn to sing, one brain connection at a time
medical.summary: A young zebra finch learning to sing may not sound like much at first, just a babbling stream of chirps and whistles. But scientists at Duke University School of Medicine say that behind the seemingly random chatter is a highly organized process that may help explain how many of us learn to do something hard, whether speaking, shredding on a guitar or mastering a new dance step.

medical.title: Peppermint oil can lower blood pressure, clinical trial finds
medical.summary: Daily doses of peppermint oil have been proved to lower blood pressure for patients with mildly high readings, new research has found. A team of University of Lancashire academics discovered a daily intake of 100 microliters of peppermint oil, taken twice a day over 20 days, lowered the systolic blood pressure (the top number in a blood pressure reading) by an average of 8.5mmHg.

medical.title: Understanding the health paradox: Study explores factors influencing white men's well-being
medical.summary: While social and economic factors are often viewed as primary drivers of health, a new analysis in the American Journal of Public Health (AJPH) suggests that the relationship between societal position and physical well-being is more complex than previously understood.

medical.title: Comparing AI anatomy segmentation models when ground truth is missing
medical.summary: As large medical imaging datasets become widely available, researchers are increasingly turning to artificial intelligence to extract useful information from scans that were never manually annotated. Automated "anatomy segmentation" tools—programs that label organs and structures in images such as CT scans—promise to make large-scale studies feasible. But as many new models appear, an important question remains: how can researchers compare these tools when no ground truth exists?

medical.title: Wearable polygraph tracks hidden stress through five body signals in real time
medical.summary: Northwestern University engineers have developed a small, wireless polygraph system you can wear. Unlike polygraphs used in television crime dramas, this wearable version isn't optimized to detect lies. Instead, engineers and physicians designed it to sense underlying stress hidden deep within the body—no interrogation room required.

medical.title: New rules for used prosthetic feet could curb 'medical equipment graveyards'
medical.summary: Researchers have proposed new standards into the decades-old prosthetic donations market, improving the quality of lower limb prosthetic feet by two-thirds—a major quality of life boost for recipients.

medical.title: Immune protein emerges as possible target to slow Parkinson's progression
medical.summary: Monoclonal antibodies can block a key immune-related protein that drives the spread of brain cell damage in Parkinson's disease (PD). This protein, called glycoprotein nonmetastatic melanoma B (GPNMB), might be part of a promising strategy for developing a treatment that slows disease progression at its earliest stages, according to a study published in Neuron, by researchers at the Perelman School of Medicine at the University of Pennsylvania.

medical.title: Routine scans may reveal tumor aggressiveness in head and neck cancer—without additional tests
medical.summary: Medical imaging routinely used in cancer care may hold far more biological information than previously thought. An international study involving Umeå University guest professor Lukas Kenner shows that PET/CT scans can capture the molecular activity of particularly aggressive head and neck tumors, opening new possibilities for more precise diagnosis and treatment planning.

medical.title: Realistic 'mock' samples created to speed cervical cancer test development
medical.summary: A team of Rice University bioengineers has developed a new way to create highly realistic "mock" patient samples that could help accelerate the development of faster, more accessible cervical cancer screening tests for low-resource settings.

medical.title: Scientists just discovered what coffee is really doing to your gut and brain
medical.summary: Coffee doesn’t just energize—it actively reshapes the gut and mind. Researchers found that both caffeinated and decaf coffee altered gut bacteria in ways linked to better mood and lower stress. Decaf even improved learning and memory, while caffeine boosted focus and reduced anxiety. Together, they show coffee works through multiple pathways beyond just caffeine.

science.title: Astronomers directly detect how turbulence between stars distorts light
science.summary: Astronomers led by the Center for Astrophysics | Harvard & Smithsonian (CfA) have made the first direct detection of turbulence distorting light in the interstellar medium. The findings will help scientists achieve clearer imaging of the supermassive black hole at the center of the Milky Way galaxy.

science.title: The first domesticated horses: 6,000 years of a complex story
science.summary: Horses were being ridden, worked, and traded long before anyone thought it possible. New research pushes back the accepted timeline of human use of horses by centuries, showing that humans used horses in organized ways as early as the 4th millennium BCE, if not earlier. The research is published in the journal Science Advances.

science.title: A new method for improving ecological monitoring in intermittent rivers
science.summary: Current indices for assessing the biological status and quality of rivers are designed to study perennial rivers, so they are not suitable for temporary rivers. This is because the lack of a constant flow of water in the riverbed affects the connectivity and dispersal of organisms, thereby reducing species diversity for natural rather than anthropogenic reasons.

science.title: Rise Goes to Washington
science.summary: “Rise,” the Artemis II zero gravity indicator, is seen sitting on the dais as NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, and Christina Koch, and CSA (Canadian Space Agency) astronaut Jeremy Hansen speak with congressional staff, Tuesday, May 12, 2026, in Washington. NASA’s Artemis II mission took Wiseman, Glover, Koch, and Hansen on a nearly 10-day […]

science.title: TESS reveals fullest night-sky map yet, with nearly 6,000 exoplanet worlds
science.summary: NASA's TESS (Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite) has released its most complete view of the starry sky to date, filling in gaps from previous observations. Nearly 6,000 colored dots scattered across the image show the locations of either confirmed or candidate exoplanets—worlds beyond our solar system—identified by the mission as of September 2025 at the end of TESS's second extended mission.

science.title: To create change, new leaders should read the room
science.summary: When Ted Lasso became the coach of last-place AFC Richmond on a popular television show, he jumped in with a can-do coaching style that ignited a team ready for change. Like Lasso, new leaders are more likely than their predecessors to improve motivation and organizational performance—but only if employees already believe change is needed.

science.title: How water fleas detect their predators
science.summary: Daphnia, also known as water fleas, are artists of defense. When their predators live nearby, the water fleas change their body structure to make themselves more difficult to eat. Professor Linda Weiss from Ruhr University in Bochum, Germany and her team have identified a chemoreceptor gene family that encodes the corresponding receptors, and is thus involved in detecting predators' signals.

science.title: Small seabirds rely on crosswinds to navigate the open ocean
science.summary: Storm petrels are among the smallest and most mysterious seabirds. Until recently, the use of biologgers to track their movements was impossible. A new study published in Biology Letters reveals that they routinely travel hundreds of kilometers while deliberately seeking crosswinds, an unexpected strategy that slows their flight but may help them survive above the open ocean.

science.title: A history of containers, an ancient technology hundreds of thousands of years in the making
science.summary: We hardly give them a second thought, but everyday objects like bags and backpacks belong to a long technological tradition that may stretch back hundreds of thousands of years.

science.title: More selective breeding might help flat-faced dogs to breathe easier
science.summary: Breeding programs could alter heritable dog characteristics to reduce the rates of brachycephalic obstructive airway syndrome (BOAS), a breathing disorder common in dogs such as Bulldogs, French Bulldogs and Pugs—according to a new study led by Dr. Joanna Jadwiga Ilska of the Royal Kennel Club, published May 13, 2026 in the open-access journal PLOS One.

science.title: Slower winds help grasslands enhance carbon gain while saving water, study finds
science.summary: Grasslands, covering 40% of Earth's vegetated surface, play a crucial role in the global carbon balance but are increasingly threatened by climate-driven water scarcity. A new study published in Science Advances finds, however, that a widespread wind speed decline—a phenomenon known as "terrestrial stilling"—is enhancing the ability of global grasslands to absorb more carbon while minimizing water loss. This shift offers a crucial buffer for these water-limited biomes under climate change.

science.title: Neanderthal dentists used stone drills to treat cavities nearly 60,000 years ago, ancient molar suggests
science.summary: Neanderthals had the know-how to identify a tooth infection and the motor skills to drill out the damage, according to a study published May 13, 2026, in the open-access journal PLOS One by Alisa Zubova of Peter the Great Museum of Anthropology and Ethnography, Russian Academy of Sciences (Kunstkamera), St. Petersburg, and colleagues.

science.title: Prehistoric Danish people continued to eat fish and hunt even after the rise of agriculture, study indicates
science.summary: Agriculture reached the coast of southern Denmark around 4000 BCE, but these prehistoric Scandinavians continued to fish and hunt too, according to a study published in PLOS One by Daniel Groß from the Museum Lolland-Falster, Denmark, Sofie Folsach Hellerøe from Aarhus University, Denmark, and colleagues.

science.title: Recreating dying stars reveals hydrogen's key role in cosmic dust formation
science.summary: Silicon carbide (SiC) dust is one of the most important ingredients in cosmic dust, the tiny particles floating throughout the cosmos that eventually give rise to new planets and stars. This compound of silicon and carbon is forged in the atmospheres of dying stars, especially carbon-rich ones, but exactly how has long remained a mystery.

science.title: AI models reveal hidden climate patterns behind US winter precipitation
science.summary: Artificial intelligence is beginning to transform climate science, not just by improving forecasts, but by helping researchers understand the physical forces shaping the planet's future.

science.title: AI tool boosts imperfect antibiotic candidates, with 85% working in lab tests
science.summary: Researchers at the University of Pennsylvania have developed ApexGO, a novel, AI-powered method for turning promising but imperfect antibiotic candidates into more potent ones. Unlike many existing AI approaches to antibiotic discovery, which screen large databases for molecules that might work, ApexGO starts with a small number of imperfect candidates and improves them step by step, using a predictive algorithm to evaluate each modification and guide the next.

science.title: Microalgae can photosynthetically produce and secrete biofuel precursors
science.summary: Microalgae have attracted growing attention as a promising platform for sustainable biofuel production because they can use photosynthesis to convert carbon dioxide into energy-rich compounds without competing with food crops. However, practical use of algal biofuels has long faced major obstacles.

science.title: Geologists in films are the good guys... but they often die
science.summary: It all began with a perfectly ordinary chat over coffee between four researchers. How many films featuring geologists can we think of? Quite quickly, the colleagues were able to come up with about 10 films. But then the scientific mind of one of them sprang into action.

science.title: Quantum geometry provides theoretical limits on measurable properties of solids
science.summary: Two RIKEN physicists have established new theoretical limits for experimentally measurable quantities by viewing solids through a lens of quantum geometry. Their results shed light both on the physics of solids and on quantum mechanics.

science.title: Liquid crystals enable on‑demand skyrmion formation at room temperature
science.summary: Researchers have recently found a new way to summon useful structures in magnetic materials using light, heat, and electric fields. This new method, described in a new study published in Physical Review Letters, may lead to more energy-efficient and flexible technologies for data storage and optical devices.

science.title: NASA Outlines Preliminary Artemis III Mission Plans
science.summary: NASA is moving quickly to define next year’s Artemis III mission in Earth orbit, a crewed flight that will test rendezvous and docking capabilities between the agency’s Orion spacecraft and commercial landers from Blue Origin and SpaceX. Since a February announcement adding an Artemis mission ahead of crewed landing missions to the Moon’s South Pole region, […]

science.title: Studying Pneumonia in Space for Heart Health on Earth
science.summary: Expedition 74 astronauts aboard the International Space Station are uncovering how bacteria that causes pneumonia can lead to long-term damage in the heart. Researchers are leveraging the space environment to observe how stem cell derived heart tissues respond to bacterial infections, to discover new methods to manage cardiovascular health and infectious diseases. In space, bacteria […]

science.title: NASA’s Planet-Hunting TESS Reveals Dazzling Night Sky
science.summary: NASA’s TESS has released its most complete view of the starry sky to date

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.summary: Primăria Capitalei anunţă, miercuri, că magistrala de metrou M3, Anghel Saligny - Preciziei, va fi prelungită, în vestul Capitalei, spre Centura Capitalei, Chiajna şi Cartierul Latin. Potrivit sursei citate, municipalitatea Bucureştiului se asociază cu Metrorex, Primăria Sectorului 6 şi Ministerul Transporturilor pentru realizarea lucrărilor. Protocolul a fost aprobat miercuri de consilierii generali, la iniţiativa primarului Sectorului 6, Ciprian Ciucu.

mix_items.summary: Fundația Calea Victoriei organizează luni, 25 mai 2026, de la ora 18:00, un nou tur ghidat dedicat Bucureștiului interbelic și patrimoniului său Art Deco și modernist, alături de Ovidiu Neacșu… Articolul Tur ghidat organizat de Fundația Calea Victoriei | Descoperă Bucureştiul Art Deco şi arhitectura modernistă apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Vești proaste pentru Sibiu! Oltenii veniți la finala Cupei vor rămâne definitiv în oraș după meci
mix_items.summary: Lovitură cruntă pentru sibieni! Orașul lor a fost luat cu asalt de câteva mii de olteni, fani ai unei echipe din Craiova veniți să asiste la finala Cupei României de diseară. Problema este însă următoarea: toți acești olteni vor rămâne definitiv în oraș după terminarea meciului. După ce că deja mai mulți olteni decât și-ar […] The post Vești proaste pentru Sibiu! Oltenii veniți la finala Cupei vor rămâne definitiv în oraș după meci appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Muzeul Național de Istorie Naturală „Grigore Antipa”” și Moon Bazar vă invită pe 16 și 17 mai 2026 la două zile dedicate copiilor, imaginației și distracției în aer liber, în grădina Muzeului Antipa!
mix_items.summary: Unde: Grădina Muzeului Antipa (Piața Victoriei) Pe cât: 16 – 17 mai 2026 10:00 – 20:00 Ce îi așteaptă pe cei mici? -Atelier creativ de confecționat corali Octyplay: copiii își… Articolul Muzeul Național de Istorie Naturală „Grigore Antipa”” și Moon Bazar vă invită pe 16 și 17 mai 2026 la două zile dedicate copiilor, imaginației și distracției în aer liber, în grădina Muzeului Antipa! apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Coadă la bătaie
mix_items.summary: The post Coadă la bătaie appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Urban Playfield, festivalul sporturilor luate în joacă, revine în mijlocul orașelor din România
mix_items.summary: Urban Playfield, festivalul sporturilor luate în joacă, revine în 2026 cu o nouă ediție a caravanei naționale care transformă spațiile urbane în terenuri temporare dedicate mișcării, sportului și jocului. Ajuns… Articolul Urban Playfield, festivalul sporturilor luate în joacă, revine în mijlocul orașelor din România apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Eroul lui Alex. Mărturia lui Mihail Soare, salvatorul care l-a zărit pe copil cu coada ochiului, din elicopter: Nici nu putea să plângă
mix_items.summary: Alex, copilul dispărut în pădurea din județul Sibiu și căutat ore întregi de sute de salvatori, a fost găsit în viață după ce un membru al echipajului elicopterului care survola zona a observat „cu coada ochiului” ceva albastru într-un luminiș. Mihail Soare, salvatorul care a participat la intervenție, a povestit într-un interviu acordat Digi24 cum o verificare făcută cu camera cu termoviziune a confirmat că este vorba despre băiatul dispărut.

mix_items.title: AI Act & AI Competencies for Leaders: De la reglementare la gândire critică responsabilă
mix_items.summary: Liderii de business de la Cluj au participat la un eveniment organizat de GetFrankly și Legal Accelerators pentru a dezbate conformarea cu EU AI Act și competențele esențiale în era… Articolul AI Act & AI Competencies for Leaders: De la reglementare la gândire critică responsabilă apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 CFR a dublat viteza trenurilor după ce a uns toate șinele cu slănină
mix_items.summary: Privită de mulți drept o companie mai degrabă înapoiată și puternic impregnată de comunism, CFR Călători le-a dat tuturor peste nas și a venit peste noapte cu o inovație care probabil va revoluționa domeniul feroviar. Câteva mii dintre zecile de mii de angajați CFR au muncit o noapte întreagă și au uns cu slănină toate […] The post CFR a dublat viteza trenurilor după ce a uns toate șinele cu slănină appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat
mix_items.summary: Tiana are 21 de ani și face voluntariat de aproximativ 3 ani în programele și activitățile World Vision România. A crescut... Articolul Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: Locație: București Fii adultul care poate schimba viitorul unui copil Dacă ai răbdare, empatie și îți dorești un job cu adevărat valoros, în care fiecare zi contează pentru dezvoltarea unor... The post Psihopedagog/Pedagog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.summary: Robert are 18 ani și este reprezentant al copiilor în Consiliul Consultativ al Copiilor și Tinerilor din cadrul World Vision România. Prin... Articolul Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: Locație: București Contract: Part time (remote) Credem în cauza SOS Satele Copiilor și știm că fiecare copil merită să crească într-o familie iubitoare. De 30 de ani SOS Satele Copiilor... The post Agent telemarketing (team leader) appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Subiecte Evaluarea Națională 2026, clasa a II-a: Elevii au susținut proba de citire la Limbă și comunicare. Când se anunță rezultatele
mix_items.summary: Elevii de clasa a II-a au început în această săptămână Evaluarea Națională 2026, odată cu susținerea probei scrise la Limbă și comunicare, organizată marți, urmată miercuri de testarea competențelor de citire. Probele s-au desfășurat în timpul orelor de curs și au avut o durată de 45 de minute.

mix_items.summary: Alexandru are 10 ani, locuiește într-un sat din județul Giurgiu și este elev în clasa a III‑a. Este un copil cu... Articolul Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: Programul național Telefonul Vârstnicului – 0800 460 001 Sprijinul media a venit de la Spot TV – Pro TV, TVR, Antena 1, Antena 3 CNN, Antena Stars, Prima TV, Prima News, Pro Cinema, Pro Arena, Acasă, Acasă Gold, Trinitas TV, Speranța TV, Antena 3 Deva,... Post-ul SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025 apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Astronomers directly detect how turbulence between stars distorts light
mix_items.summary: Astronomers led by the Center for Astrophysics | Harvard & Smithsonian (CfA) have made the first direct detection of turbulence distorting light in the interstellar medium. The findings will help scientists achieve clearer imaging of the supermassive black hole at the center of the Milky Way galaxy.

mix_items.title: Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București!
mix_items.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Un drum construit prin educație Simona este elevă în clasa a XI-a, la specializarea Tehnician în industria alimentară, la un... Articolul Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: The first domesticated horses: 6,000 years of a complex story
mix_items.summary: Horses were being ridden, worked, and traded long before anyone thought it possible. New research pushes back the accepted timeline of human use of horses by centuries, showing that humans used horses in organized ways as early as the 4th millennium BCE, if not earlier. The research is published in the journal Science Advances.

mix_items.title: Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale
mix_items.summary: București, aprilie 2026 – Într-o lume în care cererea de hrană crește accelerat, iar presiunea asupra resurselor naturale este tot... Articolul Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Psiholog
mix_items.summary: Locație: București | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: A new method for improving ecological monitoring in intermittent rivers
mix_items.summary: Current indices for assessing the biological status and quality of rivers are designed to study perennial rivers, so they are not suitable for temporary rivers. This is because the lack of a constant flow of water in the riverbed affects the connectivity and dispersal of organisms, thereby reducing species diversity for natural rather than anthropogenic reasons.

mix_items.title: „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase.
mix_items.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
mix_items.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Living 'tumor on a chip' could give best ever insight into aggressive brain cancer
mix_items.summary: Scientists are creating a glioblastoma 'tumor on a chip'—a tiny living system capable of mimicking the key features of the human brain and providing a deeper understanding of how the aggressive brain cancer works.

mix_items.title: Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești”
mix_items.summary: United Way România lucrează acolo unde nevoia este cea mai mare, acolo unde sprijinul nu este doar util, ci vital. Fără acest ajutor, mulți copii ar rămâne în urmă, riscând să se piardă în dificultățile vieții. De aceea, apreciem sprijinul pe termen lung pe care Raiffeisen Bank îl oferă programului „Învață să reușești”. Printre copiii […] Articolul Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești” apare prima dată în United Way Romania .

mix_items.title: Rise Goes to Washington
mix_items.summary: “Rise,” the Artemis II zero gravity indicator, is seen sitting on the dais as NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, and Christina Koch, and CSA (Canadian Space Agency) astronaut Jeremy Hansen speak with congressional staff, Tuesday, May 12, 2026, in Washington. NASA’s Artemis II mission took Wiseman, Glover, Koch, and Hansen on a nearly 10-day […]

mix_items.title: Alzheimer's therapeutic advancements may be financially inaccessible
mix_items.summary: A newly published study shows that while disease-modifying therapies (DMTs) may offer substantially greater health benefits than usual care, their real-world impact will depend on whether they are priced in ways that health systems can afford.

mix_items.title: TESS reveals fullest night-sky map yet, with nearly 6,000 exoplanet worlds
mix_items.summary: NASA's TESS (Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite) has released its most complete view of the starry sky to date, filling in gaps from previous observations. Nearly 6,000 colored dots scattered across the image show the locations of either confirmed or candidate exoplanets—worlds beyond our solar system—identified by the mission as of September 2025 at the end of TESS's second extended mission.

mix_items.title: To create change, new leaders should read the room
mix_items.summary: When Ted Lasso became the coach of last-place AFC Richmond on a popular television show, he jumped in with a can-do coaching style that ignited a team ready for change. Like Lasso, new leaders are more likely than their predecessors to improve motivation and organizational performance—but only if employees already believe change is needed.

mix_items.title: How water fleas detect their predators
mix_items.summary: Daphnia, also known as water fleas, are artists of defense. When their predators live nearby, the water fleas change their body structure to make themselves more difficult to eat. Professor Linda Weiss from Ruhr University in Bochum, Germany and her team have identified a chemoreceptor gene family that encodes the corresponding receptors, and is thus involved in detecting predators' signals.

mix_items.title: How songbirds learn to sing, one brain connection at a time
mix_items.summary: A young zebra finch learning to sing may not sound like much at first, just a babbling stream of chirps and whistles. But scientists at Duke University School of Medicine say that behind the seemingly random chatter is a highly organized process that may help explain how many of us learn to do something hard, whether speaking, shredding on a guitar or mastering a new dance step.

mix_items.title: Peppermint oil can lower blood pressure, clinical trial finds
mix_items.summary: Daily doses of peppermint oil have been proved to lower blood pressure for patients with mildly high readings, new research has found. A team of University of Lancashire academics discovered a daily intake of 100 microliters of peppermint oil, taken twice a day over 20 days, lowered the systolic blood pressure (the top number in a blood pressure reading) by an average of 8.5mmHg.

mix_items.title: Small seabirds rely on crosswinds to navigate the open ocean
mix_items.summary: Storm petrels are among the smallest and most mysterious seabirds. Until recently, the use of biologgers to track their movements was impossible. A new study published in Biology Letters reveals that they routinely travel hundreds of kilometers while deliberately seeking crosswinds, an unexpected strategy that slows their flight but may help them survive above the open ocean.

mix_items.title: Understanding the health paradox: Study explores factors influencing white men's well-being
mix_items.summary: While social and economic factors are often viewed as primary drivers of health, a new analysis in the American Journal of Public Health (AJPH) suggests that the relationship between societal position and physical well-being is more complex than previously understood.

mix_items.title: A history of containers, an ancient technology hundreds of thousands of years in the making
mix_items.summary: We hardly give them a second thought, but everyday objects like bags and backpacks belong to a long technological tradition that may stretch back hundreds of thousands of years.

mix_items.title: Comparing AI anatomy segmentation models when ground truth is missing
mix_items.summary: As large medical imaging datasets become widely available, researchers are increasingly turning to artificial intelligence to extract useful information from scans that were never manually annotated. Automated "anatomy segmentation" tools—programs that label organs and structures in images such as CT scans—promise to make large-scale studies feasible. But as many new models appear, an important question remains: how can researchers compare these tools when no ground truth exists?

mix_items.title: More selective breeding might help flat-faced dogs to breathe easier
mix_items.summary: Breeding programs could alter heritable dog characteristics to reduce the rates of brachycephalic obstructive airway syndrome (BOAS), a breathing disorder common in dogs such as Bulldogs, French Bulldogs and Pugs—according to a new study led by Dr. Joanna Jadwiga Ilska of the Royal Kennel Club, published May 13, 2026 in the open-access journal PLOS One.

mix_items.title: Slower winds help grasslands enhance carbon gain while saving water, study finds
mix_items.summary: Grasslands, covering 40% of Earth's vegetated surface, play a crucial role in the global carbon balance but are increasingly threatened by climate-driven water scarcity. A new study published in Science Advances finds, however, that a widespread wind speed decline—a phenomenon known as "terrestrial stilling"—is enhancing the ability of global grasslands to absorb more carbon while minimizing water loss. This shift offers a crucial buffer for these water-limited biomes under climate change.

mix_items.title: Wearable polygraph tracks hidden stress through five body signals in real time
mix_items.summary: Northwestern University engineers have developed a small, wireless polygraph system you can wear. Unlike polygraphs used in television crime dramas, this wearable version isn't optimized to detect lies. Instead, engineers and physicians designed it to sense underlying stress hidden deep within the body—no interrogation room required.

mix_items.title: Neanderthal dentists used stone drills to treat cavities nearly 60,000 years ago, ancient molar suggests
mix_items.summary: Neanderthals had the know-how to identify a tooth infection and the motor skills to drill out the damage, according to a study published May 13, 2026, in the open-access journal PLOS One by Alisa Zubova of Peter the Great Museum of Anthropology and Ethnography, Russian Academy of Sciences (Kunstkamera), St. Petersburg, and colleagues.

mix_items.title: New rules for used prosthetic feet could curb 'medical equipment graveyards'
mix_items.summary: Researchers have proposed new standards into the decades-old prosthetic donations market, improving the quality of lower limb prosthetic feet by two-thirds—a major quality of life boost for recipients.

mix_items.title: Prehistoric Danish people continued to eat fish and hunt even after the rise of agriculture, study indicates
mix_items.summary: Agriculture reached the coast of southern Denmark around 4000 BCE, but these prehistoric Scandinavians continued to fish and hunt too, according to a study published in PLOS One by Daniel Groß from the Museum Lolland-Falster, Denmark, Sofie Folsach Hellerøe from Aarhus University, Denmark, and colleagues.

mix_items.title: Recreating dying stars reveals hydrogen's key role in cosmic dust formation
mix_items.summary: Silicon carbide (SiC) dust is one of the most important ingredients in cosmic dust, the tiny particles floating throughout the cosmos that eventually give rise to new planets and stars. This compound of silicon and carbon is forged in the atmospheres of dying stars, especially carbon-rich ones, but exactly how has long remained a mystery.

mix_items.title: AI models reveal hidden climate patterns behind US winter precipitation
mix_items.summary: Artificial intelligence is beginning to transform climate science, not just by improving forecasts, but by helping researchers understand the physical forces shaping the planet's future.

mix_items.title: AI tool boosts imperfect antibiotic candidates, with 85% working in lab tests
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the University of Pennsylvania have developed ApexGO, a novel, AI-powered method for turning promising but imperfect antibiotic candidates into more potent ones. Unlike many existing AI approaches to antibiotic discovery, which screen large databases for molecules that might work, ApexGO starts with a small number of imperfect candidates and improves them step by step, using a predictive algorithm to evaluate each modification and guide the next.

mix_items.title: Microalgae can photosynthetically produce and secrete biofuel precursors
mix_items.summary: Microalgae have attracted growing attention as a promising platform for sustainable biofuel production because they can use photosynthesis to convert carbon dioxide into energy-rich compounds without competing with food crops. However, practical use of algal biofuels has long faced major obstacles.

mix_items.title: Immune protein emerges as possible target to slow Parkinson's progression
mix_items.summary: Monoclonal antibodies can block a key immune-related protein that drives the spread of brain cell damage in Parkinson's disease (PD). This protein, called glycoprotein nonmetastatic melanoma B (GPNMB), might be part of a promising strategy for developing a treatment that slows disease progression at its earliest stages, according to a study published in Neuron, by researchers at the Perelman School of Medicine at the University of Pennsylvania.

mix_items.title: Routine scans may reveal tumor aggressiveness in head and neck cancer—without additional tests
mix_items.summary: Medical imaging routinely used in cancer care may hold far more biological information than previously thought. An international study involving Umeå University guest professor Lukas Kenner shows that PET/CT scans can capture the molecular activity of particularly aggressive head and neck tumors, opening new possibilities for more precise diagnosis and treatment planning.

mix_items.title: Realistic 'mock' samples created to speed cervical cancer test development
mix_items.summary: A team of Rice University bioengineers has developed a new way to create highly realistic "mock" patient samples that could help accelerate the development of faster, more accessible cervical cancer screening tests for low-resource settings.

mix_items.title: Geologists in films are the good guys... but they often die
mix_items.summary: It all began with a perfectly ordinary chat over coffee between four researchers. How many films featuring geologists can we think of? Quite quickly, the colleagues were able to come up with about 10 films. But then the scientific mind of one of them sprang into action.

mix_items.title: Quantum geometry provides theoretical limits on measurable properties of solids
mix_items.summary: Two RIKEN physicists have established new theoretical limits for experimentally measurable quantities by viewing solids through a lens of quantum geometry. Their results shed light both on the physics of solids and on quantum mechanics.

mix_items.title: Liquid crystals enable on‑demand skyrmion formation at room temperature
mix_items.summary: Researchers have recently found a new way to summon useful structures in magnetic materials using light, heat, and electric fields. This new method, described in a new study published in Physical Review Letters, may lead to more energy-efficient and flexible technologies for data storage and optical devices.

mix_items.title: NASA Outlines Preliminary Artemis III Mission Plans
mix_items.summary: NASA is moving quickly to define next year’s Artemis III mission in Earth orbit, a crewed flight that will test rendezvous and docking capabilities between the agency’s Orion spacecraft and commercial landers from Blue Origin and SpaceX. Since a February announcement adding an Artemis mission ahead of crewed landing missions to the Moon’s South Pole region, […]

mix_items.title: Studying Pneumonia in Space for Heart Health on Earth
mix_items.summary: Expedition 74 astronauts aboard the International Space Station are uncovering how bacteria that causes pneumonia can lead to long-term damage in the heart. Researchers are leveraging the space environment to observe how stem cell derived heart tissues respond to bacterial infections, to discover new methods to manage cardiovascular health and infectious diseases. In space, bacteria […]

mix_items.title: NASA’s Planet-Hunting TESS Reveals Dazzling Night Sky
mix_items.summary: NASA’s TESS has released its most complete view of the starry sky to date

mix_items.title: Scientists just discovered what coffee is really doing to your gut and brain
mix_items.summary: Coffee doesn’t just energize—it actively reshapes the gut and mind. Researchers found that both caffeinated and decaf coffee altered gut bacteria in ways linked to better mood and lower stress. Decaf even improved learning and memory, while caffeine boosted focus and reduced anxiety. Together, they show coffee works through multiple pathways beyond just caffeine.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Am zis ca fac sport. Am facut: am fugit de responsabilitati.

satire_ro.title: Restructurarea STB provoacă haos după ce mai mulți vatmani au fost dați afară din mers
